title: unsafe get_unchecked in at() helper skips bounds checks; would be UB if indices were wrong.
description: The `at()` helper calls `plane.get_unchecked(r * stride + c)` without any bounds assertion. In the current single-threaded context the caller's clamped coordinates keep it in-range, but the absence of a debug_assert makes future correctness regressions silent UB rather than a caught panic. There is no aliasing issue today because `demosaic_rggb` reads from `raw` and writes to a separate `rgb` allocation; the `unsafe` is therefore sound under present usage. If rayon row-level parallelism is added later, the read-only shared reference to `raw` across threads is still sound, but any accidental write alias would immediately become a data race.

title: Row-level demosaic loop is structurally parallel-safe and ready for rayon if COOP/COEP headers are enabled.
description: The `chunks_mut(width * 3).enumerate().for_each(...)` pattern in `demosaic_rggb` is a sequential iterator today (no rayon in scope). Each row closure reads only from the shared immutable `raw` slice and writes exclusively to its own disjoint `out_row` chunk, so there is zero inter-row data dependency. Switching `chunks_mut(...).enumerate()` to `par_chunks_mut(...).enumerate()` via `wasm-bindgen-rayon` would be safe with no algorithmic changes. The one prerequisite is replacing `unsafe { get_unchecked(...) }` in `at()` with a debug-asserted version or switching back to safe indexing to avoid any risk of a data race being masked by UB.

title: Per-pixel tonemap loop in process() is embarrassingly parallel and safe to rayon-ize.
description: The main pixel loop in `process()` iterates `0..n` with each pixel read and write touching a disjoint index into `rgb16` and `out`. There are no shared mutable accumulators and no inter-pixel dependencies. The three pre-LUT tables (`pre_r`, `pre_g`, `pre_b`) and `post` LUT are read-only after construction and could be shared across rayon threads via a shared reference without any synchronization. Adding `par_chunks_exact_mut(3)` on `out` paired with `par_chunks_exact(3)` on `rgb16` would be idiomatic and fully safe.

title: Rotation helpers (rotate_90_cw, rotate_90_ccw, rotate_180) use chunks_mut but are sequential; safe to parallelize.
description: All three rotation helpers split `dst` into tile-row bands via `chunks_mut(TILE * w_dst * 3).enumerate().for_each(...)`. Each band reads a disjoint region of `src` (a shared immutable reference) and writes exclusively to its own `band` slice. The tile decomposition already exists specifically to improve cache locality, so converting to `par_chunks_mut` would require no algorithmic changes. The `src` reference would need to be moved into the closure with a `move` capture, which is safe since `&[u8]` is `Sync`.

title: take_rgb() silently returns empty Vec on second call; no guard prevents double-consume from JS
description: take_rgb() uses std::mem::take, draining self.rgb to an empty Vec. A second JS call returns a zero-length Uint8Array with no error, no panic, and no indication anything went wrong. The same pattern applies to take_rgb16_lb() and take_rgb16_thumb(). In worker.js the three take_* calls are sequential within a single event handler (lines 149, 186, 192) so double-call is currently safe, but there is no structural enforcement: if JS ever calls take_rgb() again (e.g. in a reprocess path that reuses a cached result object, or a copy-paste error), the caller receives a 0-byte buffer that silently produces a black image. A consumed flag or a Result return would make misuse detectable.

title: downscale_rgb panics on division-by-zero when dst_w or dst_h is 0
description: downscale_rgb validates that src.len() == sw * sh * 3 but does not reject dst_w == 0 or dst_h == 0. The first computation xr = sw as f32 / dw as f32 produces +infinity when dw == 0 (f32 division by zero, not a panic), but the subsequent loop 'for dy in 0..dh' (dh == 0) simply skips â€” so the output is an empty Vec which is also silent misbehaviour. However if dst_w == 0 but dst_h > 0, the loop runs, computing x1 = x1.min(sw).max(x0 + 1) where x0 = (dx as f32 * inf) as usize which can be usize::MAX, and the inner index (y * sw + x) * 3 on src will panic with an index-out-of-bounds in debug, or produce UB-adjacent wrap in release. Both cases should be caught with an explicit early error return.

title: downscale_rgb16_impl (internal) also divides by dw/dh without zero-check; callers must guarantee non-zero dims
description: downscale_rgb16_impl computes xr = sw as f32 / dw as f32 and yr = sh as f32 / dh as f32 without any guard. It is private and called from process_orf after lb_w and lb_h are derived via integer arithmetic guarded by .max(1), so in the current call sites the risk is low. However the absence of a local guard means any future caller or a refactor that breaks the .max(1) guarantees upstream would silently produce corrupt output or a runtime panic with no error message. The analogous public function downscale_rgb (line 370) carries the same omission.

title: wb_mode returns 0xFFFF as absent-sentinel with no documented convention; JS must know this magic value
description: When the TIFF parser does not find a WhiteBalance2 tag, wb_mode is set to 0xFFFF (line 352: info.wb_mode.unwrap_or(0xFFFF)). The JS consumer correctly handles this (worker.js line 179: result.wb_mode === 0xFFFF ? null : result.wb_mode) but this is a bespoke sentinel shared implicitly between two files in different languages. No comment in lib.rs documents that 0xFFFF means 'absent', and no comment on the JS side explains why that exact constant was chosen. An out-of-band documentation change or copy-paste of the JS consumer code without the sentinel knowledge would cause 65535 to appear in the UI as a valid WB mode. A cleaner design would expose a has_wb_mode: bool field alongside wb_mode: u16 (mirroring the has_gps pattern already present in the struct).

title: apply_look silently falls back to built-in matrix when color_matrix_flat.len() != 9; contract is undocumented
description: The comment on apply_look (line 405-406) says 'pass a slice of len != 9 to use the built-in fallback', which documents the happy-path opt-out but not the error case: if JS accidentally passes a 6- or 12-element array because of a serialisation bug, the function succeeds silently with wrong colours rather than returning an Err. The JS caller (worker.js applyLookToState) passes state.colorMatrix, a Float32Array captured via result.color_matrix_used() which always returns exactly 9 elements, so the current call site is safe. But the API surface is public (#[wasm_bindgen]), so any other future caller could pass an arbitrarily-sized slice without a clear error signal. Consider returning Err when length is neither 0 nor 9.

title: apply_look accepts width/height from JS without validating they match the rgb16_bytes length
description: apply_look receives width and height as independent u32 parameters from the JS caller and uses them to call apply_unsharp_masks and apply_orientation. If the caller passes dimensions inconsistent with rgb16_bytes.len() (e.g. a stale width/height after a resize, or a transposed dimension), the Rust code will silently operate on the wrong geometry: apply_unsharp_masks iterates height rows of width pixels over whatever slice was provided, potentially producing garbled output or an index panic. A simple guard â€” if rgb16_bytes.len() != (width as usize) * (height as usize) * 6 { return Err(...) } â€” would catch this at the boundary.

title: apply_orientation silently passes through unrecognised EXIF orientation values including the transposed variants 2, 4, 5, 7
description: The match in apply_orientation handles only values 3, 6, and 8. All other values, including EXIF-spec values 2 (flip horizontal), 4 (flip vertical), 5 (transpose), and 7 (transverse), fall through to the default arm which copies the buffer unchanged. The orientation value is passed directly from the TIFF parser without any normalisation. Olympus bodies in practice never produce values 2, 4, 5, or 7, and the fallback is safe for value 1 (Normal). However the contract is undocumented: the function signature gives no indication that only {1, 3, 6, 8} are handled. Both call sites (lib.rs and apply_look) pass the stored orientation field without further validation, and the JS lightbox info panel already displays all 8 orientation labels implying they are all expected to be handled.

title: rgb_to_rgba correctly handles empty input (produces empty output) but truncates trailing partial pixel silently
description: rgb_to_rgba computes n = rgb.len() / 3 using integer division. If the caller passes a buffer whose length is not a multiple of 3 (e.g. a slice offset error on the JS side), the trailing 1 or 2 bytes are silently discarded. For a zero-length input n == 0 and out is empty â€” this is safe and correct. The concern is the partial-pixel truncation: 7 bytes in produces 2 full RGB pixels and discards the 7th byte, which is a silent data loss contract. Given this is a public WASM export there is no enforcement that rgb.len() % 3 == 0.

title: OrfInfo is constructed with zero-valued width/height/strip_offset before validation; intermediate state could confuse future callers
description: OrfInfo is initialised with width: 0, height: 0, strip_offset: 0, strip_byte_count: 0 (lines 58-84) and only validated after the IFD loop (lines 108-116). The bail! on line 109 correctly prevents the struct from escaping in an invalid state from parse(). However OrfInfo is pub and its fields are pub, so any future code that constructs an OrfInfo directly (or holds a reference mid-parse) would have access to these zero-valued fields. There is no #[non_exhaustive] or builder pattern to prevent that. The current usage is safe, but the pattern is fragile: adding a second call site that constructs OrfInfo manually would silently bypass the validation.

title: process_orf does not validate that width or height are non-zero before allocating and indexing buffers
description: After tiff::parse returns, process_orf casts info.width and info.height to usize and immediately passes them to decompress() (which allocates width*height u16s) and demosaic_rggb() (which asserts raw.len() == width * height). tiff::parse already rejects width == 0 or height == 0 with bail!, so in practice these will always be positive. However the guard is in a different module and the safety relies on that cross-module contract being maintained. If tiff::parse were ever modified to return Ok with zero dimensions in some edge case (e.g. a new code path), decompress would allocate a zero-length buffer and demosaic would assert-fail with a panic inside WASM (producing an opaque JS error). An explicit check in process_orf itself would make the contract local and self-documenting.

title: Absent rational EXIF fields (exposure, fnumber, focal_length) use 0/0 as sentinel; JS interprets denominator==0 as absent but this is an undocumented cross-language convention
description: When exposure, fnumber, or focal_length are absent, their numerator and denominator are both set to 0 (e.g. exposure_num: info.exposure.map(|(n,_)|n).unwrap_or(0)). JS interprets denominator == 0 as absent (worker.js lines 172-173: result.exposure_den > 0 ? {...} : null). This implicit sentinel is consistent today but is not documented in either file and relies on both sides agreeing that 0/0 means 'no value'. A denominator of 0 on a present rational would also be silently treated as absent rather than surfacing as a parse error. The has_gps: bool field shows that the codebase already knows the clean pattern; rationals deserve the same treatment or at minimum a comment.
code_excerpt:
exposure_num: info.exposure.map(|(n, _)| n).unwrap_or(0),
        exposure_den: info.exposure.map(|(_, d)| d).unwrap_or(0),
        fnumber_num:  info.fnumber.map(|(n, _)| n).unwrap_or(0),
        fnumber_den:  info.fnumber.map(|(_, d)| d).unwrap_or(0),
        focal_length_num: info.focal_length.map(|(n, _)| n).unwrap_or(0),
        focal_length_den: info.focal_length.map(|(_, d)| d).unwrap_or(0),

title: No machine-readable schema or contract test documents the WASM public API surface
description: The public WASM API (process_orf, apply_look, downscale_rgb, rgb_to_rgba, ProcessResult) is consumed by JavaScript in worker.js but there is no TypeScript type declaration file (.d.ts), no JSON Schema, and no contract test that exercises the boundary with invalid inputs. The wasm-bindgen toolchain can auto-generate .d.ts stubs, and adding even a minimal set of JS-side contract tests (e.g. 'apply_look with wrong matrix length falls back gracefully', 'take_rgb twice returns empty second time') would catch regressions at the API boundary before they reach users.

title: take_rgb / take_rgb16_lb / take_rgb16_thumb lack unit tests for the double-consume edge case
description: The destructive take_* methods have no Rust unit tests verifying that a second call returns an empty buffer rather than panicking, and no JS-side tests verifying that the worker does not accidentally call them twice. Adding #[cfg(test)] mod tests with explicit double-consume assertions would serve as executable documentation of the intended contract and would catch any future refactor that changes the mem::take approach.

title: assert_eq! panics on dimension mismatch, propagating as an unrecoverable WASM trap instead of a clean JS error.
description: Line 27 calls `assert_eq!(raw.len(), width * height)`, which triggers a Rust panic if the slice length does not match. In a WASM build, a panic becomes an untrapped `unreachable` instruction that aborts the entire module instance rather than returning a JS-visible error. The caller in lib.rs already returns `Result<ProcessResult, JsError>` and validates other preconditions â€” this path should follow the same pattern and return an `Err` instead of aborting.

title: width * height multiplication can silently overflow on 32-bit WASM, making the assert pass with wrong dimensions and enabling unsafe get_unchecked OOB reads.
description: WASM runs in a 32-bit address space; `usize` is 32 bits. If `width` and `height` both come from an ORF file (e.g., 65535 Ã— 65535), `width * height` wraps to a small value in debug mode (panic) but to a wrong value in release mode (no overflow check). If the decompress step happens to return a vec of that wrong length, the assert passes. Subsequent `unsafe get_unchecked` accesses in `at()` (line 13) then read out-of-bounds memory. The fix is to use `width.checked_mul(height)` and return an error on overflow.

title: width == 0 or height == 0 causes usize underflow on (width - 1) and (height - 1), producing isize::MAX as w_max/h_max.
description: If `width` or `height` is 0, the subtraction `width - 1` underflows to `usize::MAX` in release mode (wrapping sub), which is then cast to `isize::MAX`. The `assert_eq!` on line 27 only checks `raw.len() == width*height`; when both are 0, `raw.len()` is 0 and `0*0 == 0`, so the assert passes. The `clamp` calls in `at()` will then clamp to `h_max = isize::MAX`, which is an out-of-bounds index into the raw slice. tiff.rs validates width != 0 before calling demosaic, but `demosaic_rggb` has no such self-defence.

title: Compressed input shorter than HEADER_SKIP silently returns a zero-filled blank image instead of an error.
description: When `compressed.len() <= HEADER_SKIP` (7 bytes), `decompress()` returns `vec![0u16; width * height]` with no indication that decoding was skipped. The caller in `lib.rs` uses this blank buffer for the full demosaic+tone pipeline, producing a pitch-black image with no error message surfaced to the user. This makes data-corruption or truncated-file scenarios invisible. The function should return `Result<Vec<u16>, String>` and propagate the error.

title: read_ifd allocates Vec::with_capacity(count) where count is an untrusted u16 from the file, enabling ~1.3 MB allocations per IFD call.
description: The IFD entry count is a file-controlled u16 (0â€“65535). `Vec::with_capacity(count)` allocates memory for up to 65535 `IfdEntry` structs (~20 bytes each â‰ˆ 1.3 MB) before any bounds checks are performed. `read_ifd` is called for IFD0, the EXIF sub-IFD, and the GPS sub-IFD; the MakerNote sub-IFD parser also iterates an untrusted count. A crafted file with count=65535 in each IFD triggers several multi-MB allocations. While individual read operations are bounds-checked, the upfront allocation is unbounded and can be used for a memory-exhaustion DoS. A reasonable cap (e.g., 1000 entries) should be enforced before the `with_capacity` call.

title: as_ascii computes value_off + count as u32 which can overflow, producing a smaller-than-expected usize and silently slicing the wrong region of the file.
description: In `as_ascii`, the expression `(self.value_off + self.count) as usize` adds two `u32` fields that both come from an untrusted file. On overflow (e.g., value_off=0xFFFF_FFF0, count=32), the sum wraps to a small u32 like 15, making the slice `[0xFFFF_FFF0..15]` which `r.data.get()` resolves to an empty `&[]`, returning an empty string â€” a silent wrong result. The fix is `self.value_off.checked_add(self.count)` with an early-return on overflow.

title: Inline cnt==2 WB level branch in parse_image_processing_subifd always calls val.to_le_bytes() regardless of file endianness, producing wrong WB values on big-endian ORF files.
description: When `cnt == 2`, both SHORT values fit inline in the 4-byte TIFF value field. The code calls `val.to_le_bytes()` unconditionally to split the field into two bytes pairs. On a big-endian ORF file (`r.le == false`), the `val` u32 was decoded with `u32::from_be_bytes`, but is then re-split as if it were little-endian, producing byte-swapped R and B gain values. The subsequent `r_lvl > 0 && b_lvl > 0` guard may still pass, silently storing wrong white-balance gains. The fix is to use `if r.le { val.to_le_bytes() } else { val.to_be_bytes() }` consistently, or to use `r.u16(p)` / `r.u16(p+2)` from the inline position.

title: Public WASM export downscale_rgb does not validate dst_w or dst_h, causing f32 divide-by-zero (producing Inf/NaN) when either is zero.
description: `downscale_rgb` is a `#[wasm_bindgen]` export accepting `dst_w: u32` and `dst_h: u32` from JS without checking for zero. If either is 0, the expressions `sw as f32 / dw as f32` or `sh as f32 / dh as f32` produce `f32::INFINITY`. Subsequent operations like `(dy as f32 * yr) as usize` with `yr = INFINITY` produce either 0 (for dy=0) or `usize::MAX` (saturating cast on WASM for NaN/Inf), which could then be used in slice indexing. The internal `downscale_rgb16_impl` is only called from `process_orf` where the calling code guarantees non-zero dimensions, but the exported function has no such guarantee.

title: sw * sh * 3 can overflow usize on 32-bit WASM in the downscale_rgb length check, causing the guard to incorrectly pass.
description: The length validation `src.len() != sw * sh * 3` multiplies three `usize` values on a 32-bit WASM target. If `src_w = 50000` and `src_h = 30000`, then `sw * sh = 1_500_000_000` which is within u32 range, but `* 3 = 4_500_000_000` overflows 32-bit usize (wraps to ~205 million). A crafted call with a short `src` slice that happens to equal the wrapped value bypasses the guard and passes a too-short buffer into the box-filter loop, causing out-of-bounds reads on `src[i]`, `src[i+1]`, `src[i+2]`. Use `sw.checked_mul(sh).and_then(|n| n.checked_mul(3))` and return an error on overflow.

title: at() uses get_unchecked with coordinates computed through isize arithmetic; an overflow in the calling dimension setup would produce out-of-bounds unsafe access.
description: `at()` calls `plane.get_unchecked(r * stride + c)` where `r` and `c` are `usize` values produced by clamping `isize` coordinates. The safety relies entirely on the correctness of `w_max`/`h_max` and the clamp calls at every call site, with no fallback. As documented in findings errors-g7h8i9 and errors-d4e5f6, if `width == 0` or the dimension multiplication overflows, the clamp bounds are wrong and `get_unchecked` will access memory past the slice end â€” undefined behaviour in Rust, and silent data corruption or memory disclosure in release WASM. The `unsafe` call should either be eliminated (use `plane[r * stride + c]` with a panic on internal logic errors) or protected with a debug-mode assertion that the index is in bounds.

title: Errors reading the EXIF or GPS sub-IFD are silently discarded, suppressing any indication of file corruption in those sections.
description: Both `read_ifd(&r, exif_offset)` and `read_ifd(&r, gps_offset)` are wrapped in `if let Ok(...)` with the `Err` branch silently ignored. This is intentional for a best-effort parser, but it means a partially-corrupt file (e.g., a valid IFD0 but a truncated EXIF block) produces no warning; the caller gets partial metadata with no indication that EXIF parsing failed. For a local tool this is low severity, but adding a diagnostic log or storing a parse-warnings field in `OrfInfo` would make debugging easier.

title: as_rational_triplet reads 24 bytes starting at value_off without checking that value_off + 24 is within the file, relying solely on Reader::u32 returning Err on OOB.
description: The six `r.u32(p + N)` calls are individually bounds-checked via `Reader::u32`, so no OOB memory access occurs. However, `p = self.value_off as usize` â€” on 32-bit WASM, `value_off` (a u32) cast to usize is fine, but `p + 20` could theoretically wrap if `p` is near `usize::MAX`. In practice this is unreachable (file data.len() limits p), but the potential partial result (e.g., n0/d0 read OK but n2/d2 return Err â†’ whole function returns None) is handled correctly. This is a low-severity latent issue worth noting for completeness.

title: All errors are unstructured strings; no error taxonomy or typed error enum is used anywhere in the codebase.
description: The `tiff::Result<T>` alias uses `String` as the error type, and errors are produced via a `bail!` macro that calls `format!`. This means callers cannot programmatically distinguish a 'file too small' error from a 'missing required tags' error or a 'compression not supported' error â€” they can only display the string. Introducing a typed `enum ParseError { FileTooSmall, MissingTag(&'static str), UnsupportedCompression(u16), OutOfBounds(usize) }` would enable structured error handling and cleaner JS error codes.

title: BitReader::read_bits and read_huff subtract from self.nbits without checking for underflow, which would panic in debug or wrap in release.
description: `read_bits` calls `self.fill(n)` which ensures `self.nbits >= n`, then subtracts `n`. `read_huff` calls `self.fill(12)` then subtracts `len` (the code length from the Huffman table). If the Huffman table entry has a `len` value of 0 (possible if `build_huff` is ever modified and the `huff[0]` sentinel is wrong), `self.nbits` could underflow. Currently `build_huff` produces lengths 1â€“12 so this is not a practical bug, but the invariant is implicit. Adding a debug_assert that `len >= 1` inside `read_huff` would make the invariant explicit.

title: No structured logging or diagnostics channel exists; debug information is currently emitted via JS console.log on the JS side only.
description: The Rust WASM code has no logging infrastructure (no `web-sys console::log` calls, no `log` crate, no `tracing`). Diagnostic information (e.g., which WB path was taken, which IFD tags were found, parse warnings) is completely invisible on the Rust side. Adding `web_sys::console::log_1` calls or the `log` crate with a WASM adapter (`console_log`) would make it possible to diagnose field issues without recompiling with custom debug builds.

title: strip_end computed as usize addition can silently overflow on wasm32
description: On wasm32, `usize` is 32-bit. `info.strip_offset as usize + info.strip_byte_count as usize` can overflow if both values are large (e.g. strip_offset near 2 GB), producing a small wrapped value that passes the `strip_end > data.len()` bounds check even though the true end is out of range. A malformed or adversarial ORF can exploit this to pass the safety check and allow subsequent slice indexing to produce an incorrect slice, potentially causing incorrect output orâ€”depending on the allocatorâ€”a panic. The fix is to use `u32::checked_add` on the raw u32 values before casting, or `usize::checked_add` and map the None case to an error.

title: Running-average update `diff * 3` can overflow i32
description: `diff` is computed as `(acarry[parity][0] ^ sign) + acarry[parity][1]` where both operands are i32. `acarry[parity][0]` is `(high << nbits) | literal_bits`; with high up to 4094 and nbits up to 15, it can be ~134 million, making `diff` similarly large. Multiplying by 3 in `(diff * 3 + acarry[parity][1]) >> 5` then silently overflows i32 (max ~2.1 billion; 3 Ã— 134M = 402M, which fits, but if two accumulations compound, wrap is possible for pathological streams). The overflow does not panic in release mode (Rust wraps in release), corrupting the running average and producing wrong pixel predictions for all subsequent pixels in the column parity lane until a row reset. dcraw uses `int` with the same expression, so the bug mirrors dcraw, but is still a defect.
code_excerpt: acarry[parity][1] = (diff * 3 + acarry[parity][1]) >> 5;

title: Inline SHORT[2] handling in parse_image_processing_subifd uses unconditional to_le_bytes(), breaking BE files
description: When `cnt == 2` the code reconstructs the two inline SHORTs from `val` by calling `val.to_le_bytes()` unconditionally, then conditionally re-interprets with `u16::from_le_bytes` or `u16::from_be_bytes`. However `val` was already decoded from the file by `r.u32(e + 8)` which applied the file endianness, producing a host-native integer. Calling `to_le_bytes()` on a native integer and then reading the bytes as BE when `r.le == false` reverses the bytes a second time, swapping R and B WB gains and producing inverted color balance for any BE-endian ORF (magic bytes `MMOR`/`MMMR`). On LE files (all Olympus ORFs in practice) the result is correct. The correct approach is to directly extract halves from `val` using bit shifts, as already done consistently everywhere else in the file (e.g. the `inline_u16` closure in `parse_olympus_makernote`).

title: IfdEntry::as_u32 treats value_off as a file pointer for dtype==3, count==2 (inline case)
description: TIFF spec Â§7 states that when `count * sizeof(type) <= 4`, the value is stored inline in the value_offset field, not as a pointer. For `dtype == 3` (SHORT, 2 bytes each), `count == 2` means 4 bytes total â€” all inline. `as_u32` handles `count == 1` inline correctly, but for `count > 1` it falls through to `r.u16(self.value_off as usize)`, treating `value_off` as a file pointer. Any caller that uses `as_u32` on a SHORT[2] entry will dereference an arbitrary file offset (the packed value of the two SHORTs) instead of reading the actual data. In practice IFD0 tags resolved via `as_u32` (width, height, orientation, etc.) all have count==1 so this path is currently not triggered by the parser; however it is a latent correctness defect if new tags are added or files with non-standard count==2 for those tags are encountered.

title: Sub-IFD offset calculation uses unchecked u32 addition that can overflow
description: In `parse_olympus_makernote`, for sub-IFDs 0x2010, 0x2020, and 0x2040, the absolute offset is computed as `base_off as u32 + val`. Both `base_off` and `val` are derived from file offsets (each fitting comfortably in u32 for files under 4 GB), but their sum could exceed u32::MAX for a crafted file, wrapping silently and causing the sub-IFD parse to read from a wrong location (likely producing garbage metadata rather than a panic, since bounds checks exist in the readers). Confidence is medium because real ORF files are small enough that overflow is unreachable in practice.

title: Huffman escape path: extra bits = 16 - nbits, but when nbits >= 16 saturating_sub gives 0, silently outputting high=0
description: When `high0 == 12` (escape code), the code reads `extra = (16u32).saturating_sub(nbits as u32)` bits. `nbits` can reach 16 (starting at 2+i and growing in the while loop up to the guard `nbits < 16` â€” but the loop body does `nbits += 1` so nbits exits the loop at exactly 16 when carry is large). At nbits==16, `saturating_sub` returns 0, `read_bits(0)` returns 0, and the escape value becomes 0 after the right-shift. This silently drops the escape payload instead of reading the expected number of bits, corrupting pixel values for any row with a high carry value that triggers the escape code. dcraw reads `(16 - nbits)` unconditionally; when nbits==16 that expression is 0 in C too, so the behaviors match â€” but it means escape at nbits==16 always yields 0, which may be an inherited dcraw quirk worth verifying against real ORF data.

title: apply_unsharp_mask clamps to 4095 but rgb16 values can be full u16 range after apply_look path
description: In `apply_unsharp_mask`, the result is clamped to `0..4095`, which is correct when processing freshly demosaiced 12-bit sensor data (max raw value 4095). However, `apply_unsharp_masks` is also called from `apply_look` in lib.rs with a `rgb16` buffer that was unpacked from cached lightbox bytes â€” those bytes were produced from the same demosaiced data, so the values are still at most 4095. The clamp is therefore safe today, but the function signature accepts any `&mut [u16]` and the clamp upper bound is a hardcoded magic number with no documentation. If the pipeline is ever extended to process HDR or 14-bit data, the clamp silently destroys values 4096â€“65535. A named constant or a parameter would make the intent explicit and avoid a future regression.

title: No property-based or round-trip tests on the Huffman decompressor math-heavy code
description: The decompressor contains several interacting stateful components (carry state, Huffman table, predictor, bit reader) with tricky edge cases at row boundaries, escape codes, and carry overflow. There are no unit tests or property-based tests (e.g. via proptest) verifying that known-good pixel patterns survive a round-trip or that specific dcraw reference inputs produce expected outputs. Given that pixel value bugs are silent (output looks plausible but is subtly wrong), fuzz or property tests comparing against a reference implementation would provide high confidence. Similarly, `pipeline.rs` math (tone curve, color matrix, saturation) is untested, making it hard to detect regressions from slider logic changes.

title: Inline-vs-pointer discrimination for TIFF value fields is duplicated across multiple parse functions
description: The logic for deciding whether a TIFF entry's value is inline (count * sizeof(type) <= 4) or a file pointer is scattered across `IfdEntry::as_u32`, `parse_olympus_makernote` (the `inline_u16` closure), `parse_image_processing_subifd`, and the camera-settings parser. Each site makes slightly different decisions about which counts are inline (e.g. `cnt <= 2` vs `cnt == 2` vs `cnt == 1`), creating inconsistencies and potential future bugs. A single `fn tiff_value_bytes(entry, reader) -> Result<SmallVec<[u8;4]>>` or similar helper that centralises the inline/pointer dispatch would eliminate the duplication and the discrepancy in the SHORT[2] case found in issue `logic-a3b4c5d6`.

title: separable_blur allocates two full-image buffers and has an inner kernel loop that defeats LLVM auto-vectorization
description: For a 20MP image with the 13-tap clarity kernel, separable_blur executes ~780M multiply-adds across two passes (H-pass then V-pass), each touching widthÃ—heightÃ—3Ã—13 iterations. The innermost loop iterates over the kernel with a per-iteration clamped index calculation `(x as isize + ki as isize - half as isize).clamp(...)` that introduces a data-dependent address computation per tap, preventing LLVM from emitting SIMD gather/load sequences. Two `vec![0u16; n]` allocations of ~120MB each (for a 20MP image: 5472Ã—3648Ã—3Ã—2 bytes = ~120MB) occur on every blur call. When both texture and clarity are active, four such allocations happen back-to-back.

title: process() rebuilds three 65536-entry pre-LUTs and one 65536-entry post-LUT on every apply_look call, totalling ~384KB of computation at interactive slider rates
description: Each call to `process()` invokes `build_pre_lut` three times and `build_post_lut` once. Each LUT iterates 65536 entries: the pre-LUT does two float multiplies and a clamp per entry; the post-LUT calls `tone_curve` which contains up to three `powf` calls (shadows gamma, highlights gamma, sRGB EOTF). At 60fps drag on a lightbox image, `apply_look` calls `process` on every animation frame, re-evaluating all four LUTs each time regardless of which slider moved. The powf calls inside `build_post_lut` are especially costly: at 65536 iterations with potential 3 powf each, that is up to ~196K `powf` calls per frame.

title: apply_unsharp_masks calls separable_blur twice, allocating and immediately discarding up to four full-image buffers (~480MB total for 20MP)
description: When both texture and clarity are non-zero, `apply_unsharp_masks` calls `apply_unsharp_mask` twice, each of which calls `separable_blur`, which itself allocates two `Vec<u16>` of `width * height * 3` elements. For a 20MP image that is 4 Ã— 120MB = ~480MB of allocation and deallocation per re-render triggered by slider drag. These buffers cannot be reused across the two calls because each has different dimensions (same here, since full-image) but the blur intermediates are discarded after the sharpening blend. The allocations also stress WASM's linear memory, which cannot release pages back to the OS.

title: Channel-interleaved loop order in separable_blur prevents auto-vectorization across channels
description: The innermost loop structure is `y â†’ x â†’ c â†’ ki`, where `c` iterates over 3 channels inside the pixel loop. This interleaving means each channel's accumulation is mixed with index computations for the other channels, preventing LLVM from widening across the 3-channel dimension. Reordering to `c â†’ y â†’ x â†’ ki` (with planar intermediate buffers) would allow the `x` inner loop to be a contiguous SIMD-friendly reduction. With WASM SIMD (128-bit, 4 f32 lanes), the kernel accumulation loop over 5 or 13 taps could be vectorized across 4 output pixels at once, giving a theoretical 4x speedup on the inner loop.

title: downscale_rgb16_impl recomputes floating-point row/column bounds (x0, x1, y0, y1) per output pixel using f32 arithmetic that is not hoisted out of the column loop
description: In `downscale_rgb16_impl`, the vertical span `y0` and `y1` depends only on `dy` and `yr`, so it could be computed once per row. However, `x0` and `x1` recompute `dx as f32 * xr` and `((dx as f32) + 1.0) * xr` per pixel. For a 5472Ã—3648 â†’ 1800Ã—1200 downscale, the outer loop runs 1200 times and the inner loop 1800 times, performing 3600 f32 multiplies for bounds that could be integer-incremented. The `y0..y1` and `x0..x1` inner loops also recompute the flat index `y * sw + x` without hoisting the `y * sw` stride, causing an extra multiply per source pixel in the box.

title: apply_look unpacks rgb16 bytes into a Vec<u16> using a per-element map/collect, missing a safe bytemuck or copy_from_slice path
description: The `apply_look` function unpacks `rgb16_bytes` (6 bytes per pixel, packed u16 LE) into `Vec<u16>` using `(0..n).map(|i| u16::from_le_bytes([rgb16_bytes[i*2], rgb16_bytes[i*2+1]])).collect()`. This generates an iterator that accesses two non-adjacent bytes per step and builds a Vec element-by-element, with bounds checks on each pair. A `bytemuck::cast_slice` (zero-copy reinterpret on LE platforms, which WASM is) or a `u16::from_le_bytes` loop with `chunks_exact(2)` would be more efficient and still safe. For a 1800Ã—1200 lightbox buffer that is 2.16M elements being individually converted on every slider drag.

title: rgb_to_rgba pushes one byte at a time rather than using extend_from_slice or unsafe ptr copy
description: The `rgb_to_rgba` function pushes 4 bytes per pixel individually into a pre-capacity Vec. While LLVM will likely vectorize 4 sequential `push` calls on a Vec with spare capacity, the explicit `push` API still goes through the Vec growth boundary check on each call. Using `out.extend_from_slice(&[r, g, b, 255])` or building the array inline and writing 4 bytes at once via a `chunks_mut(4)` approach is cleaner and more reliably vectorized. For a 20MP image this is 20M pixels Ã— 4 pushes = 80M individual calls through the Vec::push code path.

title: process() inner pixel loop computes luma and vibrance weighting with floating-point per pixel even when vibrance is zero
description: For every pixel, `process` computes `luma`, `raw_mx`, `mx`, `mn`, `pixel_sat`, and `vib_w` unconditionally, regardless of whether `vibrance` (or `params.vibrance`) is zero. When vibrance is 0, `vib_w` is unused in the final scale (`vib * vib_w * 0.6` = 0), making min/max/saturation computation dead work. For a 20MP image that is 20M iterations of `r2.max(g2).max(b2)` + `r2.min(g2).min(b2)` + one divide and one clamp that could be skipped with a simple branch on `vib != 0.0`. Similarly, saturation applies a per-pixel luma computation every time even when `sat == 1.0` (no-op scale).

title: build_post_lut calls tone_curve 65536 times, each potentially calling powf up to 3 times, totalling up to ~196K powf calls per process() invocation
description: Inside `build_post_lut`, `tone_curve` is called for each of 65536 LUT entries. `tone_curve` conditionally calls `powf` for shadows gamma, highlights gamma (both guarded by `abs() > 1e-4`), and the sRGB EOTF (`linear_to_srgb` which calls `powf(1.0/2.4)` for non-tiny values). In the common case (shadows != 0, highlights != 0, most values > 0.0031308), all three fire: ~196K `powf` calls per build. `powf` in wasm32 is a software fallback (no native instruction), making it significantly more expensive than on native x86 with `vrsqrtss` or hardware approximations. A fixed-point polynomial approximation or a two-segment approximation for the sRGB EOTF would eliminate most of this cost.

title: LUTs in process() are pure functions of params and could be cached across apply_look calls when params are unchanged
description: The four LUTs (`pre_r`, `pre_g`, `pre_b`, `post`) are entirely determined by `PipelineParams`. On every slider drag event, `apply_look` is called with params that typically differ in only one field (e.g. contrast changes the post-LUT but not the pre-LUTs; WB changes only the pre-LUTs). Caching the LUTs on the JS side as `Uint16Array` / `Uint8Array` blobs and passing them in, or moving LUT construction into a separate WASM export (`build_luts(params) -> LutHandle`), would allow `apply_look` to skip LUT rebuild when the relevant params are unchanged. This is the highest-leverage single change for interactive responsiveness.

title: process() inner pixel loop is a good candidate for WASM SIMD via std::arch::wasm32
description: The per-pixel loop in `process` does a 3Ã—3 matrix multiply, 3-component luma dot product, and per-channel scale â€” all branchy-free floating-point that maps well to `f32x4` WASM SIMD intrinsics. The LUT lookups (scatter/gather) cannot be vectorized, but the matrix and saturation stages before the lookup could process 4 pixels at once using `v128` instructions. Given the loop runs 20M times for a full-resolution render, even a partial vectorization of the matrix stage would be impactful. This requires `wasm32` target feature `simd128` and explicit intrinsics (auto-vectorization is unlikely due to the LUT gather at the end).

title: rotate_180 copies pixels three bytes at a time instead of as a u32 or u24 word
description: The `rotate_180` function iterates columns and copies 3 bytes individually (`dst_row[c*3]`, `dst_row[c*3+1]`, `dst_row[c*3+2]`). A single `dst_row[c*3..c*3+3].copy_from_slice(&s_row[sc*3..sc*3+3])` would let LLVM emit a 3-byte or 4-byte load-store, and the reversed-column copy could also be split into a row-reversal using chunks of 3 bytes via a `rev()` chunk iterator. For a 20MP image rotated 180Â°, this is 60M byte copies. The current form is unlikely to auto-vectorize because of the non-contiguous scatter pattern (reversed column index).

title: Decompressor recomputes multiple raw[row*width + col Â± 2] index expressions redundantly for the north-west predictor
description: In the main decompression loop, when `row >= 2 && col >= 2`, three reads occur: `out[row*width + col - 2]`, `out[(row-2)*width + col]`, and `out[(row-2)*width + col - 2]`. The multiplications `row*width` and `(row-2)*width` are recomputed from scratch each iteration rather than being maintained as incremented row-offset variables. LLVM's strength-reduction should handle this, but the branch structure (three separate `if row < 2 / col < 2` guards) adds branch cost that could be resolved by precomputing `row_base = row * width` and `prev_row_base = (row - 2) * width` outside the column loop. Low severity since decompression is a one-shot operation.

title: Timing instrumentation covers only decompress/demosaic/tonemap/orient but not the two downscale_rgb16_impl calls or apply_unsharp_masks
description: The `now_ms()` timing in `process_orf` wraps decompress, demosaic, and tonemap+orient, but `downscale_rgb16_impl` (called twice, for lb and thumb) and `apply_unsharp_masks` (called before `process`) are not individually timed. Given that a 20MP â†’ 1800px downscale with a box-filter over ~9 source pixels per output pixel is ~29M pixel reads, and unsharp masks dominate at ~480MB of allocation, profiling data for these stages would be valuable for optimization prioritization.

title: strip_offset + strip_byte_count can overflow usize on wasm32, defeating the bounds guard
description: On wasm32, usize is 32 bits. Both strip_offset and strip_byte_count are u32 values read from the attacker-controlled ORF file. Their sum on line 217 is computed as usize arithmetic, so if strip_offset = 0xFFFFFFF0 and strip_byte_count = 0x20, strip_end wraps to 0x10. The guard on line 218 (`strip_end > data.len()`) then passes for any file >= 16 bytes. The subsequent slice `&data[info.strip_offset as usize..strip_end]` has start > end and Rust panics (WASM trap), producing a reliable denial-of-service. No memory unsafety occurs because the safe-Rust slice panics, but an attacker can trap the WASM worker with a crafted header.

title: get_unchecked in demosaic `at()` is safe for normal images but the assert does not prevent stride-overflow OOB on wasm32
description: The assertion `assert_eq!(raw.len(), width * height)` (line 27) and the zero-dimension guard in lib.rs together mean `at()` is safe for well-formed inputs. However, on wasm32 where usize=u32, if an attacker supplies width and height both close to 65535, then `width * height` wraps during the assert (producing the same wrapped value that `decompress` used to allocate `out`), and the assert passes. Inside the demosaic loop, `row * width + col` also wraps identically, so `get_unchecked` aliases within the allocated buffer rather than escaping it â€” no true OOB in practice. The real residual risk is that the `unsafe` block provides no auditable proof of safety without studying all callers, and any future change that modifies allocation size without updating the assert would silently introduce UB. Severity is medium because current call chains are safe, but the unsafe is structurally fragile.

title: as_ascii value_off + count addition can overflow u32, silently truncating parsed strings
description: In IfdEntry::as_ascii, the range `self.value_off as usize .. (self.value_off + self.count) as usize` computes the addition in u32 before casting. If value_off is 0xFFFFFF00 and count is 0x200, the sum wraps to 0xFF. The .get() call then returns None (start > end is caught), so the string is silently empty rather than causing a panic. While not a memory safety issue, this means crafted tag values can cause critical metadata (make/model/lens) to be silently lost, potentially bypassing downstream logic that relies on those fields. Should be computed as `(self.value_off as usize).saturating_add(self.count as usize)`.
code_excerpt:
let bytes = r
            .data
            .get(self.value_off as usize..(self.value_off + self.count) as usize)
            .unwrap_or(&[]);
        String::from_utf8_lossy(bytes)

title: base_off as u32 + val sub-IFD offset arithmetic overflows u32 in MakerNote parsing
description: In parse_olympus_makernote, sub-IFD offsets for Equipment (0x2010), CameraSettings (0x2020), and ImageProcessing (0x2040) are computed as `base_off as u32 + val` before being passed to sub-IFD parsers. base_off is a usize cast to u32 (truncating on 64-bit), and val is attacker-controlled from the ORF. If the sum overflows u32, the resulting offset is passed to the sub-IFD parser which does a `p + 2 > r.data.len()` check, but with a wrapped (small) p value the check passes and the parser reads IFD entries from near offset 0 of the file. This causes wrong data to be parsed as WB/lens metadata â€” a data-confusion rather than memory-safety issue. Each sub-parser is otherwise bounds-checked.

title: downscale_rgb length check overflows usize on wasm32 for large attacker-supplied dimensions
description: The exported WASM function downscale_rgb accepts src_w and src_h from JavaScript without upper-bound validation. On wasm32, `sw * sh * 3` can overflow usize when dimensions are large (e.g. src_w=50000, src_h=50000: 50000*50000*3 = 7.5B > 4GB). The overflow makes the length check on line 367 compare against a wrapped value, allowing it to pass with a mismatched src buffer. The inner loop then accesses `src[(y * sw + x) * 3 + 2]`, which Rust's bounds checker traps â€” producing a WASM panic rather than memory corruption, but enabling reliable DoS from JS. A similar overflow exists in the output allocation `dw * dh * 3`.

title: apply_look does not validate that rgb16_bytes length matches the declared widthÃ—height
description: The exported apply_look function accepts width and height from JavaScript and derives rgb16 by dividing rgb16_bytes.len() by 2, ignoring whether the resulting length matches width*height*3. When apply_unsharp_masks or process is called with these dimensions, separable_blur allocates `width*height*3` entries but accesses `src[(y*width+xi)*3+c]` using the caller-supplied width/height. If rgb16_bytes is shorter than the dimensions imply, these accesses panic (WASM trap). An adversarial JS caller can trivially trigger this by passing a 6-byte buffer with width=1000, height=1000. Adding a check `if rgb16.len() != w * h * 3 { return Err(...) }` before the processing stage would fix this.

title: read_ifd allocates Vec with attacker-controlled count up to 65535 entries without bounding
description: read_ifd reads a u16 entry count directly from the ORF file and calls Vec::with_capacity(count). A crafted ORF can set count=65535, causing a ~786 KB allocation per IFD. This function is called for IFD0, the EXIF IFD, and the GPS IFD â€” three potential allocations of this size per file. While each individual allocation is modest, combined with the fact that subsequent iterations attempt to read 12 bytes per entry (each bounds-checked), the main concern is CPU time for large counts pointing past end-of-file: the loop fails gracefully but iterates up to 65535 times with a bounds check each iteration. This is a low-severity DoS.

title: BitReader.fill() accumulates nbits past data end, risking u32 overflow on pathologically large images
description: When the compressed stream is exhausted, fill() returns synthetic 0 bytes and increments nbits by 8 on each call. For an attacker-crafted ORF with valid width/height (e.g. 4096x4096 = 16M pixels) but a near-empty compressed stream, the decompressor loops 16M times, each iteration calling fill() multiple times and accumulating nbits. On a 32-bit wasm target, nbits (u32) overflows after ~536M bits of phantom fill. After overflow, `self.buf >> (self.nbits - n)` computes with a wrapped nbits value; Rust's u32 subtraction wraps in release mode, and the shift amount modulo 64 produces arbitrary bit patterns. This corrupts pixel values silently rather than panicking. In practice wasm memory limits cap image sizes, so exploitation requires an extremely large declared dimension.

title: No centralized input validation schema for ORF structural constraints (dimensions, offsets, counts)
description: Validation of attacker-controlled fields (width, height, strip_offset, strip_byte_count, IFD entry counts, sub-IFD offsets) is scattered across parse(), read_ifd(), and individual IfdEntry methods, with no single place that enforces documented maximums (e.g. max image dimension 8192, max IFD entries 256). Centralizing these checks into a validation step after tiff::parse() returns would make the security surface auditable and prevent issues like the strip_end overflow and read_ifd large-count DoS. This is a structural hardening opportunity, not a single exploitable bug.

title: No SAST tooling (cargo-audit, cargo-geiger, or Clippy deny) configured in CI for this WASM binary parser
description: The repository processes untrusted binary files (ORF) in a security-sensitive context (WASM exposed to the web) but has no evidence of automated static analysis in CI. cargo-geiger would flag the unsafe block in demosaic.rs, cargo-audit would catch known-vulnerable dependency versions, and `#![deny(unsafe_code)]` at crate root (with a targeted allow for demosaic::at) would make unsafe surface auditable. Adding these as CI steps would catch regressions and new unsafe additions automatically.
